advantages: Whole-array operations run in compiled NumPy/BLAS code, dramatically reducing interpreter overhead compared to Python for-loops on large numeric data.
Broadcasting expresses shape alignment declaratively — subtract a per-feature mean from every row without explicit tiling or copying.
Vectorized code is usually shorter and closer to the mathematical notation used in ML papers (matrix minus vector, element-wise masks).
The same ndarray layout feeds directly into scikit-learn, PyTorch, and JAX, so fast prep steps do not require format conversion.
disadvantages: Broadcasting mistakes produce silent wrong answers when shapes align accidentally but not as intended — harder to debug than an explicit loop.
Very large intermediate broadcast arrays can spike memory (e.g. outer operations on big tensors) even though the expression looks compact.
Not every pipeline step vectorizes cleanly; irregular logic, heavy branching, or string data still needs Python loops, pandas, or specialized libraries.
Micro-optimizing tiny arrays can add complexity without measurable gain — vectorization pays off mainly at realistic ML batch sizes.
